levels.title: Operator
levels.summary: Uses Claude Code as a command-line tool with basic prompting and accepts output with minimal verification.
definition: The Operator treats Claude Code as a smart autocomplete or command executor. Interactions are single-turn or loosely structured. The practitioner can install, launch, and prompt Claude Code but lacks a systematic approach to task framing or output verification.
At this level, the tool is useful but unpredictable. Results vary because prompts are vague, context is not managed, and there is no feedback loop beyond 'does it look right?'

coreConcepts.name: CLI Basics
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding how to install, launch, and interact with Claude Code from the command line.

coreConcepts.name: Basic Prompting
coreConcepts.definition: The ability to express a request in natural language that Claude Code can act on.

coreConcepts.name: Tool Output Reading
coreConcepts.definition: The ability to read and interpret Claude Code's responses, diffs, and tool invocations.

coreConcepts.name: Model Awareness
coreConcepts.definition: A basic understanding that Claude Code is powered by a language model with capabilities and limitations, not a deterministic compiler.

coreConcepts.name: Permission Model
coreConcepts.definition: Understanding that Claude Code asks for permission before executing commands, editing files, or accessing resources, and why those guardrails exist.

coreSkills.name: Install and Run
coreSkills.definition: Can install Claude Code and start a session.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Launches Claude Code from terminal without assistance.

coreSkills.name: Simple Prompting
coreSkills.definition: Can write a natural language prompt that produces useful output.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Asks Claude Code to write a function and gets a working result.

coreSkills.name: Accept or Reject Output
coreSkills.definition: Can review a generated diff and decide whether to accept it.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Reviews a diff and either accepts or asks for revision.

coreSkills.name: Tool Navigation
coreSkills.definition: Can use slash commands (/help, /clear) and understand the basic tool palette.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Uses /help to discover available commands without external documentation.

coreSkills.name: Basic Error Recovery
coreSkills.definition: Can recognize when Claude Code has produced an error and retry with a different approach.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Rephrases a prompt after getting an unhelpful response rather than giving up.

observableBehaviors: Launches Claude Code and immediately starts prompting without setting context.
Accepts generated code without running tests.
Uses single-turn prompts for complex multi-step tasks.
Does not use CLAUDE.md or any persistent configuration.
failureModes: Accepts incorrect code because the output 'looks right.'
Repeats the same vague prompt when results are poor instead of refining.
Loses context in long sessions without realizing it.
Does not understand why the same prompt produces different results.
graduationCriteria: Consistently provides context before asking for code changes.
Reviews generated diffs line-by-line before accepting.
Can explain what Claude Code did and why.
Begins to decompose larger tasks into smaller prompts.

evaluationTasks.title: Generate a Utility Function
evaluationTasks.description: Ask Claude Code to write a string utility function with edge cases.
successCriteria: Practitioner can explain what it does
Practitioner reviews the diff before accepting

evaluationTasks.title: Fix a Known Bug
evaluationTasks.description: Give Claude Code a file with a known bug and ask it to find and fix the issue.
successCriteria: Practitioner verifies the fix by running the code

learningEmphasis: Focus on reading output carefully. Every diff is a learning opportunity.
Start providing context: tell Claude Code what file you're working in, what the function should do, and what constraints exist.

levels.title: Structured Collaborator
levels.summary: Structures interactions with explicit context, constraints, and verification loops.
definition: The Structured Collaborator has moved beyond naive prompting. They provide context, specify constraints, decompose tasks, and verify output systematically.
At this level, Claude Code becomes a reliable pair programmer. The practitioner understands that prompt quality directly determines output quality and invests in framing.

coreConcepts.name: Context Setting
coreConcepts.definition: Providing relevant file context, codebase conventions, and requirements before requesting changes.

coreConcepts.name: Task Decomposition
coreConcepts.definition: Breaking complex tasks into sequential, well-scoped prompts.

coreConcepts.name: Verification Loops
coreConcepts.definition: Systematically testing and reviewing output after each interaction.

coreConcepts.name: Constraint Specification
coreConcepts.definition: Explicitly stating what the output must and must not do — including edge cases, performance bounds, and style conventions.

coreConcepts.name: Iterative Refinement
coreConcepts.definition: Using follow-up prompts to correct, extend, or improve initial output rather than accepting or rejecting wholesale.

coreSkills.name: Contextual Prompting
coreSkills.definition: Can write prompts that include file paths, conventions, and constraints.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Prompts reference specific files and coding standards.

coreSkills.name: Task Decomposition
coreSkills.definition: Can break a feature request into 3-5 sequential prompts.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Plans a multi-step interaction before starting.

coreSkills.name: Systematic Verification
coreSkills.definition: Runs tests and reviews diffs after every significant change.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Never accepts a diff without running the test suite.

coreSkills.name: Error Diagnosis
coreSkills.definition: Can read Claude Code error output and provide corrective context to resolve issues.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Pastes error messages back with analysis and asks for targeted fixes.

coreSkills.name: Session Management
coreSkills.definition: Knows when to continue a session vs. start fresh to maintain context quality.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Starts a new session when context has drifted rather than fighting accumulated confusion.

observableBehaviors: Provides file paths and context at the start of each session.
Decomposes features into multiple focused prompts.
Runs tests after each change and iterates on failures.
Asks Claude Code to explain its reasoning when output seems wrong.
failureModes: Over-specifies prompts to the point of constraining useful creativity.
Spends too long on prompt engineering instead of iterating.
Does not yet use persistent project configuration.
Treats each session as independent rather than building on prior work.
graduationCriteria: Creates and maintains CLAUDE.md files for projects.
Uses hooks or pre-commit checks to enforce standards.
Configures project-level context that persists across sessions.
Begins using MCP servers or external tool integrations.

evaluationTasks.title: Multi-Step Feature Implementation
evaluationTasks.description: Implement a feature that requires changes across 3+ files using decomposed prompts.

evaluationTasks.title: Guided Refactoring
evaluationTasks.description: Refactor an existing module by providing Claude Code with constraints (preserve API, improve internal structure, add tests).
successCriteria: New tests cover the refactored code

learningEmphasis: Invest in prompt structure. A well-framed prompt saves multiple iterations.
Start exploring CLAUDE.md and project-level configuration to reduce repetitive context-setting.

levels.title: Environment Builder
levels.summary: Configures persistent project environments with CLAUDE.md, hooks, MCP, and permissions for consistent AI-assisted development.
definition: The Environment Builder shifts focus from individual prompts to persistent infrastructure. They configure CLAUDE.md files, set up hooks, integrate MCP servers, and design project structures that make Claude Code effective by default.
At this level, the practitioner's environment does much of the work. New team members can be productive with Claude Code immediately because the project is configured for it.

coreConcepts.name: CLAUDE.md Configuration
coreConcepts.definition: Using project-level and directory-level CLAUDE.md files to provide persistent context, conventions, and instructions.

coreConcepts.name: Hooks and Automation
coreConcepts.definition: Configuring pre-commit hooks, linting rules, and automated checks that run alongside Claude Code.

coreConcepts.name: MCP Server Integration
coreConcepts.definition: Setting up Model Context Protocol servers to give Claude Code access to external tools and data sources.

coreConcepts.name: Permission Configuration
coreConcepts.definition: Setting up allowlists and denylists for tool use so Claude Code can operate efficiently without excessive permission prompts.

coreConcepts.name: Layered Context Architecture
coreConcepts.definition: Designing a hierarchy of context files (root CLAUDE.md, directory-level overrides, .claude/settings) that provide the right information at the right scope.

coreSkills.name: CLAUDE.md Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design effective CLAUDE.md files that guide AI behavior for a project.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Project has layered CLAUDE.md files with build commands, conventions, and architecture notes.

coreSkills.name: Hook Configuration
coreSkills.definition: Can configure hooks that enforce code quality standards automatically.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Pre-commit hooks catch common issues before code is committed.

coreSkills.name: MCP Server Setup
coreSkills.definition: Can configure and use MCP servers for database, API, or file system access.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Claude Code accesses external systems through configured MCP servers.

coreSkills.name: Permission Tuning
coreSkills.definition: Can configure permission settings that balance safety with productivity.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Claude Code runs common operations without interrupting for approval, while still gating destructive actions.

coreSkills.name: Team Onboarding
coreSkills.definition: Can set up an environment so that a new team member can use Claude Code productively on day one.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: New contributor follows CLAUDE.md and is productive without asking how to use Claude Code in the project.

observableBehaviors: Maintains CLAUDE.md files as living documents updated with project evolution.
Configures hooks for linting, testing, and formatting.
Sets up MCP servers for project-specific external tool access.
Designs directory structures that help Claude Code navigate large codebases.
failureModes: Over-engineers CLAUDE.md with excessive detail that the model ignores.
Configures hooks that are too strict and block productive work.
Sets up MCP servers without proper security boundaries.
Does not version-control environment configuration.
graduationCriteria: Designs workflows that span multiple Claude Code sessions.
Uses non-interactive or headless mode for automated tasks.
Begins orchestrating multiple agents on parallel tasks.
Integrates Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines.

evaluationTasks.title: Project Environment Setup
evaluationTasks.description: Configure a new project with CLAUDE.md, hooks, and at least one MCP server.
successCriteria: Hooks catch at least one common issue

evaluationTasks.title: Team Environment Handoff
evaluationTasks.description: Configure an environment and have a colleague use Claude Code in the project without verbal instructions.
successCriteria: Colleague completes a task using only CLAUDE.md and project config for guidance

learningEmphasis: Think of your environment as a product. It should make Claude Code more effective for everyone who uses it.
Start exploring automation: what tasks can Claude Code do without you watching?

levels.title: Workflow Engineer
levels.summary: Designs multi-agent workflows, CI/CD integration, and automated development pipelines using Claude Code.
definition: The Workflow Engineer operates at the system level. They design workflows where multiple Claude Code instances collaborate, tasks are parallelized across agents, and AI coding is integrated into continuous integration and deployment pipelines.
At this level, Claude Code is not just a tool — it is infrastructure. The practitioner thinks about orchestration, error handling, and workflow resilience.

coreConcepts.name: Multi-Agent Orchestration
coreConcepts.definition: Running multiple Claude Code instances on related but independent tasks, coordinating their work.

coreConcepts.name: CI/CD Integration
coreConcepts.definition: Embedding Claude Code into automated pipelines for code generation, review, and testing.

coreConcepts.name: Headless Operation
coreConcepts.definition: Using Claude Code in non-interactive mode for scripted and automated workflows.

coreConcepts.name: Worktree Isolation
coreConcepts.definition: Using git worktrees to give each agent an isolated copy of the repository, preventing merge conflicts during parallel operation.

coreConcepts.name: Output Quality Monitoring
coreConcepts.definition: Designing feedback loops that detect when automated agent output drifts below acceptable quality thresholds.

coreSkills.name: Workflow Orchestration
coreSkills.definition: Can design and run multi-agent workflows with proper task decomposition.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Runs parallel agents on independent tasks and merges results.

coreSkills.name: Pipeline Integration
coreSkills.definition: Can embed Claude Code into CI/CD pipelines for automated code operations.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: CI pipeline includes Claude Code steps for code review or generation.

coreSkills.name: Workflow Error Handling
coreSkills.definition: Can design workflows that handle failures gracefully.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Automated workflows retry or escalate on failure rather than silently breaking.

coreSkills.name: Task Graph Design
coreSkills.definition: Can model dependencies between agent tasks and determine which can run in parallel.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Draws or documents a task dependency graph before launching parallel agents.

coreSkills.name: Token and Cost Management
coreSkills.definition: Can estimate and manage token usage across multi-agent workflows to control costs.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Sets token budgets for automated workflows and monitors actual usage.

observableBehaviors: Runs multiple Claude Code instances on parallel branches.
Uses headless mode for batch operations.
Integrates Claude Code into PR review automation.
Designs retry and fallback logic for automated workflows.
failureModes: Over-automates without sufficient verification checkpoints.
Creates workflows that are brittle and hard to debug.
Does not monitor automated agent output for quality drift.
Ignores security implications of autonomous code generation.
graduationCriteria: Designs organizational AI coding strategy and governance.
Creates safety boundaries and audit trails for autonomous agents.
Mentors others on AI coding best practices.
Can evaluate and adopt new AI coding tools and patterns.

evaluationTasks.title: Multi-Agent Workflow Design
evaluationTasks.description: Design and execute a workflow where 2+ Claude Code agents work on parallel tasks with coordinated output.

evaluationTasks.title: CI/CD Pipeline Integration
evaluationTasks.description: Add a Claude Code step to an existing CI pipeline that automatically reviews or generates code on pull requests.
successCriteria: Agent output is reviewed before merge

learningEmphasis: Think about AI coding as infrastructure, not a tool. What does reliability mean when agents generate code?
Start considering governance: who reviews what the agents produce? What audit trail exists?

levels.title: Agentic Systems Expert
levels.summary: Architects organizational AI coding strategy, governance frameworks, and safety boundaries for autonomous agentic systems.
definition: The Agentic Systems Expert operates at the organizational and architectural level. They design governance frameworks, safety boundaries, and strategy for how AI coding tools are adopted, scaled, and governed across teams.
At this level, the practitioner is not just using Claude Code — they are shaping how their organization uses AI for software development, including policy, risk management, and capability evaluation.

coreConcepts.name: AI Coding Governance
coreConcepts.definition: Designing policies, review processes, and audit trails for AI-generated code at organizational scale.

coreConcepts.name: Safety Boundaries
coreConcepts.definition: Defining what autonomous agents can and cannot do, with enforcement mechanisms.

coreConcepts.name: Organizational AI Strategy
coreConcepts.definition: Planning how AI coding tools are adopted, integrated, and evolved across an organization.

coreConcepts.name: AI Risk Assessment
coreConcepts.definition: Evaluating the risk profile of AI-generated code in different contexts — from internal tooling to production-critical systems.

coreConcepts.name: Human-AI Teaming
coreConcepts.definition: Designing collaboration models that optimize the strengths of both human engineers and AI agents while mitigating the weaknesses of each.

coreSkills.name: Governance Framework Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design and implement governance frameworks for AI-generated code.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Organization has documented policies for AI code review and approval.

coreSkills.name: Safety Boundary Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design safety mechanisms that prevent autonomous agents from causing harm.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Agents operate within defined boundaries with automatic escalation.

coreSkills.name: Capability Evaluation
coreSkills.definition: Can evaluate new AI coding tools and patterns for organizational fit.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Produces structured evaluations of new tools with risk/benefit analysis.

coreSkills.name: Training Program Design
coreSkills.definition: Can design onboarding and upskilling programs for AI coding competence across teams.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Team members progress through competence levels with structured guidance.

coreSkills.name: AI Coding Metrics
coreSkills.definition: Can define and track metrics that measure the effectiveness and safety of AI coding adoption.
coreSkills.observableEvidence: Dashboard tracks AI coding productivity, quality, and incident rates.

observableBehaviors: Designs organization-wide AI coding policies.
Creates audit trails and compliance mechanisms for AI-generated code.
Evaluates new AI coding tools and recommends adoption strategy.
Mentors teams on responsible AI coding practices.
Designs safety boundaries for autonomous agent operation.
failureModes: Creates governance that is too heavy and blocks productive work.
Focuses on strategy without maintaining hands-on technical skill.
Under-estimates the pace of tool evolution and locks into rigid frameworks.
Does not account for the human experience of working alongside AI agents.
graduationCriteria: This is the current ceiling of the framework. Graduation would involve contributing to the advancement of the field itself.

evaluationTasks.title: Governance Framework Design
evaluationTasks.description: Design a governance framework for AI-generated code in a team of 10+ engineers.
successCriteria: Framework covers code review, audit, permissions, and escalation

evaluationTasks.title: Organizational Adoption Plan
evaluationTasks.description: Create a phased adoption plan for introducing AI coding tools to a development organization.
successCriteria: Plan addresses training, tooling, governance, and measurement

learningEmphasis: The frontier is moving fast. Stay hands-on while developing organizational perspective.
Study how data infrastructure governance evolved — AI coding governance will follow similar patterns.
